planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after all frost danger has passed into well-drained, full-sun soil.

directions: Propagate from semi-hardwood cuttings taken in summer and root them in a well-draining mix before transplanting to a permanent site in spring. This fast-growing coastal shrub thrives in sandy, poor soils and tolerates salt spray, making it ideal for seaside gardens in warm zones. The aromatic gray-green trifoliate leaves and purple flower spikes appear through summer and have long been used in Asian herbal traditions for their fragrant essential oils. In borderline zones 8-9, plant against a south-facing wall and mulch the base heavily to protect the crown through cold snaps.

planting.note: Set out container plants after frost in well-drained soil; established plants are more cold-tolerant.

directions: Root cuttings in a moist, well-drained medium in warm conditions and transplant to the garden in spring once nighttime temperatures remain above 40 degrees F. Unlike the invasive Lantana camara, this native species is well-behaved and blooms with small white to pale lavender flower clusters nearly year-round in frost-free regions. The pungently fragrant foliage deters browsing by deer, and the blooms are a premier nectar source for swallowtails, skippers, and other butterflies. Water sparingly once established and prune lightly in late winter to shape the plant and encourage fresh flowering stems.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or divisions in spring into moist, humus-rich soil in shade.

directions: Propagate from stem-tip cuttings or layered runners, as nursery plants are more reliable than seed-grown starts, and set into cool, shaded beds in spring with consistent moisture. This low, mat-forming evergreen herb of Pacific Coast forests produces an intensely sweet mintlike fragrance that made it the aromatic plant of choice for tea among Indigenous peoples of California and the Pacific Northwest. Plant it beneath conifers or deciduous trees where it can sprawl freely as a fragrant groundcover in dappled shade with moderate, even moisture. Harvest young stems and leaves throughout the growing season to steep as a refreshing herbal tea, and divide established mats every few years to keep growth vigorous.

planting.note: Direct-sow in fall for cold stratification, or surface-sow in early spring.

directions: Surface-sow seed in fall directly in the garden, or press it lightly into a moist seedbed in early spring, as the fine seed needs light and cool temperatures to germinate well. This minty-aromatic native of Europe and western Asia thrives in lean, well-drained soils in full sun to partial shade, where it forms upright clumps bearing dense whorls of small purplish-pink flowers from June through September that are magnets for bumblebees and butterflies. The leaves can be used fresh or dried as a thyme-like culinary herb in soups, stuffings, and savory dishes. Cut plants back by half after the main flush of bloom to encourage a second flush and keep growth compact.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings in spring into sharp-draining, lean soil in full sun.

directions: Propagate from softwood cuttings taken in early summer and root them in a gritty, fast-draining mix before setting plants out in spring in their permanent location of full sun and extremely well-drained, lean soil. This exceptionally fragrant mounding subshrub is native to rocky slopes and mountain meadows of the American West, where it produces dense heads of pinkish-purple flowers in summer that are avidly worked by native bees and hummingbirds. Once established it is highly drought-tolerant and demands little supplemental water, making it an outstanding choice for xeric rock gardens and dry slopes in western gardens. Avoid overwatering or clay soils, which quickly cause root rot; treat it as a Mediterranean herb by cutting back lightly in early spring to remove winter-killed tips and encourage fresh growth.
